lesson.name: God is Dead, Nihilism & the Übermensch - Lesson 3: Master and Slave Morality

meta.courseTitle: God is Dead, Nihilism & the Übermensch
meta.lessonObjective: Trace Nietzsche's distinction between master and slave morality in Beyond Good and Evil, and examine it critically.

tasks.chapterTitle: Front Matter and Project Gutenberg Information

messages.content:
Lesson 3: Master and Slave Morality - Presentation25 Slides
Guiding Quote: Two Moralities
  • “… there is master-morality and slave-morality.”
  • Nietzsche claims that two opposed value-systems run through history: one that grows from strength and self-affirmation, and one born of weakness and ressentiment.
  • This lesson sets the two side by side – their origins, their verdicts on “good” and “evil”, and the objections they invite.
Test your understanding of this lesson:

questions.question: According to the text, what did the men of the new reversal have to do first?
options: Shatter the strong and reverse all estimates of value
questions.explanation: Direct from p.25: they had to reverse all estimates of value and shatter the strong.

questions.question: What does the text say vanity reveals about the person who feels it?
options: The presence of the slave's craftiness in his blood
questions.explanation: p.61 states vanity is the slave in the blood seeking to seduce others to good opinions.

questions.question: In the aristocratic commonwealth described, what condition produces the stable master type?
questions.explanation: p.62: the long struggle with unfavourable conditions establishes the hard, uniform type.

questions.question: What consequence does the text attribute to the victory of slave morality?
options: Rise of a new race of commanders
Production of a dwarfed, gregarious, mediocre species
questions.explanation: p.25 and p.62 both describe the resulting mediocre, gregarious European.

questions.question: Why does the text call the Christian reversal a presumption?
options: Its agents were not great or hard enough to fashion man
questions.explanation: p.25: men not great enough nor hard enough hacked and botched the finest stone.

cards.front: What task did the new moralists set themselves according to p.25?
cards.back: To reverse all estimates of value and shatter the strong.

cards.front: What concrete result followed the triumph of unworldliness?
cards.back: A dwarfed, gregarious, mediocre European species.

cards.front: What instinct remains visible in vanity?
cards.back: The slave's craftiness that seeks to seduce others to good opinions.

cards.front: Under what condition does an aristocratic type become stable and hard?
cards.back: Constant struggle with uniform unfavourable conditions.

cards.front: What does aristocratic morality count among the virtues?
cards.back: Intolerance itself, under the name of justice.

cards.front: What happens when external enemies disappear and abundance arrives?
cards.back: The old discipline severs and the individual dares to detach himself.

cards.front: What species alone has a prospect of continuing after the change?
cards.back: The incurably mediocre.

cards.front: Why does the text call the Christian project a presumption?
cards.back: Its agents lacked the greatness and hardness to fashion man as artists.

cards.front: What does master morality teach as a condition of life?
cards.back: The narrowing of perspectives and limited horizons.

cards.front: What demand of slave morality harms higher men?
cards.back: The requirement of one morality for all.

slides.title: Guiding Quote: Two Moralities
bullets: “… there is master-morality and slave-morality.”
Nietzsche claims that two opposed value-systems run through history: one that grows from strength and self-affirmation, and one born of weakness and ressentiment.
This lesson sets the two side by side – their origins, their verdicts on “good” and “evil”, and the objections they invite.

slides.title: The Reversal of Values
bullets: In the Jewish and Christian traditions a new type of man arose who had to reverse all estimates of value.
These men shattered the strong, spoiled great hopes, and cast suspicion on every autonomous, manly, conquering instinct.
slides.notes: Direct exposition from the religious mood section.

slides.title: Slave Morality's Target
bullets: Slave morality broke down everything imperious and natural to the highest type of man.
It turned love of the earthly and of supremacy over the earth into hatred of the earth and earthly things.

slides.title: The Outcome of Reversal
bullets: Under the standard of unworldliness and unsensuousness a dwarfed and mediocre species was produced.
The European of the present day became an obliging, sickly, gregarious animal.
slides.notes: Shows concrete result of slave values.

slides.title: Master Morality in Aristocratic Life
bullets: An aristocratic commonwealth is a contrivance for rearing human beings who must prevail or be exterminated.
In constant struggle with unfavourable conditions the species learns the qualities it calls virtues.

slides.title: Severity of Master Morality
bullets: Aristocratic morality is intolerant in education, marriage, penal laws, and relations of rank.
It counts intolerance itself among the virtues under the name of justice.

slides.title: The Stable Master Type
bullets: A type with few but very marked features is established: severe, warlike, reserved, reticent men.
This hardness and uniformity allow the species to prevail and remain permanent.

slides.title: When Conditions Change
bullets: When enemies disappear and means of life exist in superabundance the old discipline is no longer felt as necessary.
The bond of constraint severs and the individual dares to detach himself.

slides.title: Appearance of the Individual
bullets: At this turning-point magnificent up-growth and extraordinary decay appear side by side.
The individual must now have recourse to his own law-giving for self-preservation.
slides.notes: Moment when master morality loses its ground.

slides.title: Vanity as Slave Instinct
bullets: Vanity is an atavism in which the older propensity of the slave overmasters the younger.
The vain person rejoices over every good opinion and suffers from every bad opinion because the slave in his blood seeks to seduce others to think well of him.
slides.notes: Direct quotation and explanation of slave remnant.

slides.title: The Slave in the Blood
bullets: The slave immediately falls prostrate before the good opinions he has himself called forth.
Much of the slave still remains in woman, for instance, as the remains of the slave's craftiness.

slides.title: Master Morality's Intolerance
bullets: Every aristocratic morality implants the need for limited horizons and immediate duties.
It teaches the narrowing of perspectives and regards this narrowing as a condition of life.
slides.notes: Links back to virtues of hardness.

slides.title: Slave Morality's Universal Claim
bullets: Slave morality demands one morality for all and treats distinction of rank as a detriment.
It produces the mediocre alone, who have the prospect of continuing and propagating themselves.

slides.title: The Mediocrity That Survives
bullets: The mediocre have a prospect of continuing because they alone fit the new conditions.
Everything around them decays and produces decay; nothing will endure except this one species.

slides.title: Objection: The Cost of Reversal
bullets: The reversal required men not great enough nor hard enough to fashion man as artists.
They hacked and botched the finest stone and produced a sublime abortion instead of a higher type.

slides.title: Objection: Loss of Rank Distinctions
bullets: Men not sufficiently noble failed to see the radically different grades and intervals of rank that separate man from man.
Equality before God therefore became the instrument that swayed the destiny of Europe toward the dwarfed species.

slides.title: Objection: The Gregarious Result
bullets: The universal degeneracy of mankind to the level of the man of the future idealized by socialistic fools is now possible.
This degeneracy and dwarfing of man to an absolutely gregarious animal is the troubling consequence of slave morality's triumph.

slides.title: Tension: Master Hardness Turned Inward
bullets: When the old external struggle ends, the strength accumulated by master morality turns against the individual himself.
The same morality that bent the bow so threateningly now stands out of date and leaves the single person without common formulas.

slides.title: Tension: The New Danger Within
bullets: Great danger is shifted into the individual, the neighbour, the friend, the street, and the heart.
The moral philosophers who appear at this time discover that everything decays except the incurably mediocre.

slides.title: Critical Appraisal: Who Benefits
bullets: The requirement of one morality for all is really a detriment to higher men.
Slave morality therefore serves the preservation of the mediocre at the expense of the exceptional.

slides.title: Critical Appraisal: The Artist's Complaint
bullets: One would have to approach this degeneration with a divine hammer and cry: what have you done to my finest stone?
Christianity has hitherto been the most portentous of presumptions because it lacked the strength and far-sightedness to let the law of failures prevail.

slides.title: Master Morality's Source of Virtue
bullets: The qualities to which the aristocratic species owes its existence are called virtues and are developed with severity.
These virtues alone are allowed to reach maturity under the name of justice.
slides.notes: Returns to positive features for balance.

slides.title: Slave Morality's Psychological Root
bullets: The slave morality originates in the craftiness of those who must seduce others to good opinions of themselves.
It remains active as an atavistic instinct long after external slavery has ended.

slides.title: Quiz · Self-test
